refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and network dependency.
DP6: Establish operational resilience through human-in-the-loop monitoring, rapid incident response procedures, and documented recovery protocols.
DP7: Combine DP1 + DP3 + DP6: Redundancy for critical paths, predictive maintenance to extend mean-time-between-failures (MTBF), and operational procedures to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Achieve availability ≥0.97 through a three-pillar approach: (1) redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover for high-consequence failures; (2) condition-based predictive maintenance to extend MTBF and reduce unplanned downtime; (3) documented operational procedures, training, and rapid incident response to minimize MTTR. This composite DP addresses both design-time resilience (redundancy, predictability) and runtime resilience (human response, recovery speed).

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP1 component) and predictive maintenance (DP3 component) share sensor/data infrastructure; coupling risk if monitoring system itself becomes single point of failure.
Operational procedures (DP6 component) depend on human training and discipline; coupling to organizational capability and worksite safety culture.
Failover logic must coordinate with maintenance windows; temporal coupling between automatic recovery and planned maintenance scheduling.
Graceful degradation (DP4) and redundancy (DP1) may conflict if redundant units are not sized to handle full load; design must clarify load-sharing assumptions.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composite solution that decouples failure modes (design redundancy handles hardware faults; predictive maintenance prevents wear-out; procedures handle operational faults). However, the three pillars share common dependencies (instrumentation, data pipelines, human oversight) that must be explicitly managed. Recommend explicit interface contracts between pillars to minimize hidden coupling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints; RISK: Redundancy adds weight, power, complexity, and cost—customer acceptance required.

ASSUMPTION: Condition monitoring data is reliable and predictive models are validated for the NGHE duty cycle; RISK: Poor data quality or model drift will degrade maintenance effectiveness and availability.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite personnel are trained, equipped, and available to execute rapid response and recovery procedures; RISK: Availability depends on human performance; training gaps or staffing shortages directly impact MTTR.

ASSUMPTION: Critical subsystems can be designed with sufficient independence to enable redundancy without excessive coupling; RISK: Tight integration (e.g., shared power rails, control buses) may force redundancy of larger-than-necessary units.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to observe representative failure modes and validate 0.97 target; RISK: Short evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations.

ASSUMPTION: 'Operational availability' is defined consistently (e.g., excludes planned maintenance, includes partial degradation); RISK: Ambiguous definition will lead to disputes over acceptance criteria.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly define: (1) Availability metric scope (e.g., 'system can deliver ≥80% of rated output' vs. 'system is powered and responsive'); (2) Evaluation period duration and conditions (e.g., 'continuous operation over 30 days, excluding scheduled maintenance windows'); (3) Acceptable failure modes (e.g., 'graceful degradation to 50% capacity is acceptable; total loss is not'); (4) Maintenance strategy assumptions (e.g., 'spare parts available on-site within 4 hours'). Revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥0.97 (defined as: system delivers ≥80% rated output or is in controlled recovery mode) over a continuous 30-day evaluation period, excluding planned maintenance windows, under nominal worksite conditions and with documented spare parts and trained personnel available per the operational support plan.' This revision clarifies the DP-to-acceptance-criteria mapping and reduces ambiguity in IM (support infrastructure) requirements.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for availability assurance through redundancy management, predictive maintenance coordination, operational procedure enforcement, and incident response orchestration. Owns the three-pillar availability strategy: redundancy control logic, maintenance scheduling interfaces, and runtime resilience procedures.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health/condition data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive maintenance algorithms and real-time availability monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts and failover commands triggered by redundancy logic or maintenance scheduling decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to coordinate task suspension/resumption during failover events or maintenance windows; receive operational directives.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for distributed redundancy coordination and remote incident response support.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of availability assurance, redundancy orchestration, and incident response. Predictive maintenance scheduling and operational procedures align with safety domain. Minor diffusion: actual redundant subsystem implementations (hardware/software) reside in Vehicle/Teleoperation, but control logic and coordination stay in Safety Services.

Moderate fit if NGHE is the top-level system integrator. However, availability is a cross-cutting concern that should not be owned by a single application layer. Redundancy logic, maintenance scheduling, and incident response would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and operational teams. Overly broad and loses cohesion.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns physical redundancy (e.g., dual motors, backup power) but not the orchestration logic, predictive maintenance scheduling, or operational procedures. Availability strategy spans Vehicle, Teleoperation, and human response—too distributed. High coupling to external incident response and maintenance teams.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a functional subsystem, not a resilience owner. Availability logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Safety Services, and operational procedures. Redundancy failover and maintenance coordination are outside Teleoperation's scope.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for the three-pillar availability DP. It naturally owns: (1) redundancy orchestration logic and failover automation (safety-critical), (2) predictive maintenance scheduling and health monitoring (safety-relevant), and (3) operational procedures and incident response coordination (safety domain). The module interfaces cleanly with Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry (inbound health data), SafetyAlert (outbound failover/maintenance commands), Tasking (coordination), and Network Link (distributed resilience). Diffusion is minimal because actual redundant hardware/software implementations remain in their respective subsystems; Safety Services owns only the control and coordination layer.
containment_risks: Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle subsystem internals (e.g., motor wear patterns, battery degradation). Mitigation: Define clear Telemetry schema and health indicators; Safety Services consumes abstract health metrics, not raw sensor data.

Risk: Incident response procedures may depend on real-time operational context (e.g., mission criticality, environmental hazards) that spans Vehicle, Teleoperation, and external factors. Mitigation: Establish a formal incident response protocol owned by Safety Services that interfaces with operational teams via documented procedures and training.

Risk: Redundancy failover logic may require tight coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops for automatic switchover. Mitigation: Use SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces to decouple failover commands from low-level subsystem control; ensure failover is deterministic and testable.

Risk: MTTR minimization depends on human response speed, which is outside the system boundary. Mitigation: Safety Services owns training and procedure documentation; actual response execution is a human/operational responsibility, not a module responsibility.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP7 is well-formulated and maps cleanly to Safety Services. No revision needed. However, consider explicitly decomposing DP7 into three sub-DPs for clarity: (1) DP7a: Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover logic (owned by Safety Services, realized in Vehicle/Teleoperation); (2) DP7b: Establish condition-based predictive maintenance scheduling via health telemetry analysis (owned by Safety Services); (3) DP7c: Define and enforce operational procedures, training, and incident response protocols (owned by Safety Services, executed by human operators). This decomposition makes ownership and interface boundaries even more explicit.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability ≥0.97 is measurable over the agreed evaluation period via operational telemetry and incident logs. Safety Services module owns the orchestration and monitoring logic; actual redundancy implementations in Vehicle/Teleoperation are observable via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces. Verification requires: (1) baseline MTBF/MTTR metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, (2) predictive maintenance effectiveness tracking via health telemetry, (3) incident response time audits against documented procedures. All are traceable to Safety Services ownership.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services is the natural owner of the three-pillar availability strategy. Allocation is explicit: redundancy orchestration (Safety Services control logic), predictive maintenance (Safety Services scheduling via Telemetry), operational procedures (Safety Services documentation and training coordination). Diffusion is minimal and well-bounded—actual redundant hardware/software remains in Vehicle/Teleoperation; Safety Services owns only the coordination and decision layer. Interface contracts (Telemetry inbound, SafetyAlert outbound, Tasking bidirectional, Network Link) are well-defined and decouple Safety Services from subsystem internals.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require abstraction of Vehicle subsystem health indicators. Mitigation: Enforce strict Telemetry schema (e.g., motor_wear_index, battery_soc, thermal_margin) to prevent Safety Services from coupling to raw sensor data or Vehicle internals.

Risk: Automatic failover logic must synchronize with Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops in real-time. Mitigation: Define deterministic failover state machines and test via SafetyAlert interface contracts; ensure failover commands are idempotent and timeout-safe.

Risk: Incident response procedures depend on human operators and external teams (maintenance crews, field support). Mitigation: Clearly separate Safety Services responsibilities (procedure documentation, training coordination, incident logging) from human execution; use Tasking interface to signal operational context but not to enforce human actions.

Risk: MTTR minimization is partially outside system boundary (depends on human response speed and logistics). Mitigation: Safety Services owns procedure optimization and training; actual response time is a KPI tracked by operational teams, not a module responsibility. Document this boundary explicitly in acceptance criteria.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the cohesive owner of availability assurance. The three-pillar DP maps cleanly: (1) Redundancy orchestration is a safety-critical control function naturally owned by Safety Services; (2) Predictive maintenance scheduling is a safety-relevant decision function that consumes abstract health metrics via Telemetry; (3) Operational procedures and incident response are safety-domain responsibilities that Safety Services documents and coordinates (but does not execute). Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-managed: redundant subsystem implementations remain in Vehicle/Teleoperation, but Safety Services owns the control and coordination layer. Interface boundaries are clean and decouple Safety Services from subsystem internals.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 is well-formulated and directly addresses the FR. The three-pillar approach (redundancy, predictive maintenance, operational procedures) is comprehensive and logically sound for achieving availability ≥0.97. DP7 explicitly bridges design-time resilience (redundancy, predictability) and runtime resilience (human response, recovery speed), which is appropriate for a complex autonomous system. The DP is measurable (availability metric), traceable (three pillars are distinct and verifiable), and feasible (redundancy is proven technology; predictive maintenance is industry-standard; operational procedures are documented and trainable). No revision to DP7 itself is required.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP7 into three explicit sub-DPs for clarity and traceability: (1) DP7a: Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover logic to eliminate single points of failure in high-consequence failure modes; (2) DP7b: Establish condition-based predictive maintenance scheduling via health telemetry analysis to extend MTBF and reduce unplanned downtime; (3) DP7c: Define, document, and enforce operational procedures, training programs, and incident response protocols to minimize MTTR and ensure rapid recovery. This decomposition makes ownership (all three owned by Safety Services), interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking), and verification criteria (MTBF extension, MTTR reduction, procedure compliance) explicit.

Formalize the Telemetry interface schema to define abstract health indicators (e.g., motor_wear_index, battery_soc, thermal_margin, subsystem_status) that Safety Services consumes for predictive maintenance. This prevents Safety Services from coupling to Vehicle/Teleoperation internals and ensures maintainability.

Document the boundary between Safety Services responsibilities (procedure documentation, training coordination, incident logging, failover orchestration) and human/operational responsibilities (actual incident response execution, maintenance crew dispatch, field support). Explicitly state that MTTR is a KPI tracked by operational teams, not a module responsibility.

Define deterministic failover state machines and test them via SafetyAlert interface contracts. Ensure failover commands are idempotent, timeout-safe, and do not require tight coupling to Vehicle/Teleoperation control loops.

Establish acceptance criteria for each sub-DP: (1) DP7a: Redundancy coverage ≥95% of high-consequence failure modes; automatic failover latency ≤100ms; (2) DP7b: Predictive maintenance accuracy ≥90%; MTBF extension ≥20% vs. reactive maintenance baseline; (3) DP7c: Procedure compliance ≥95%; training completion rate ≥100%; incident response time ≤30 minutes (or customer-defined target). These criteria are measurable and traceable to Safety Services ownership.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR7 and DP7 are sound and well-formulated (all three gates pass with high confidence). However, the recommended_revisions explicitly call for decomposition of DP7 into three sub-DPs (DP7a, DP7b, DP7c) to make ownership, interface contracts, and verification criteria explicit. This is not a revision of the current level but a structured decomposition to the next level. The three-pillar approach (redundancy orchestration, predictive maintenance scheduling, operational procedures) is logically distinct and requires separate sub-FRs to enable detailed design, interface specification, and verification. High safety criticality reinforces the need for explicit traceability and clear ownership boundaries at each pillar. Decomposition is justified and necessary for detailed design and implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7a: Eliminate single points of failure in high-consequence failure modes by orchestrating automatic failover between redundant critical subsystems.
FR7b: Extend mean time between failures (MTBF) and reduce unplanned downtime by predicting subsystem health degradation and scheduling maintenance before failure.
FR7c: Minimize mean time to recovery (MTTR) and ensure rapid incident response by defining, documenting, and enforcing operational procedures, training programs, and incident response protocols.

assumptions: Safety Services module is the natural and sole owner of all three sub-DPs (redundancy orchestration, predictive maintenance scheduling, operational procedures coordination).
Telemetry interface provides abstract health indicators (motor_wear_index, battery_soc, thermal_margin, subsystem_status) that decouple Safety Services from Vehicle/Teleoperation internals.
SafetyAlert interface enables deterministic, idempotent failover commands with latency ≤100ms and timeout safety guarantees.

Tasking interface allows Safety Services to signal operational context (e.g., maintenance scheduling, incident alerts) but does not enforce human actions; human execution remains outside system boundary.
Availability ≥0.97 is measurable over an agreed evaluation period via operational telemetry, incident logs, and MTBF/MTTR metrics.
Predictive maintenance algorithms are feasible and industry-standard; accuracy ≥90% is achievable with proper health telemetry abstraction.
Operational procedures and training are documented, enforceable, and compliant with safety standards; procedure compliance ≥95% is achievable with proper governance.

MTTR is a KPI tracked by operational teams (human response speed, logistics, field support) and is not solely a Safety Services module responsibility; Safety Services owns procedure optimization and training coordination.
open_risks: Predictive maintenance algorithms may couple to raw sensor data or Vehicle subsystem internals if Telemetry schema is not strictly enforced. Mitigation: Define and enforce Telemetry schema contracts; conduct design reviews to verify abstraction boundaries.

Automatic failover logic may not synchronize correctly with Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops in real-time, causing cascading failures or unsafe states. Mitigation: Define deterministic failover state machines; test via SafetyAlert interface contracts; conduct hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing with Vehicle and Teleoperation simulators.

Incident response procedures may not be followed consistently by human operators and field support teams, leading to extended MTTR. Mitigation: Establish clear procedure documentation, training programs, and compliance audits; track procedure compliance as a KPI; conduct incident response drills.

MTTR minimization is partially outside system boundary (depends on human response speed and logistics). If operational teams lack resources or training, MTTR targets may not be met. Mitigation: Clearly document the boundary between Safety Services responsibilities and human/operational responsibilities; establish SLAs with operational teams; track MTTR as a joint KPI.

Redundancy coverage may not reach ≥95% of high-consequence failure modes if Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems lack sufficient redundancy. Mitigation: Conduct FMEA/FTA to identify high-consequence failure modes; allocate redundancy requirements to Vehicle/Teleoperation; verify coverage via design reviews and testing.

Failover latency may exceed 100ms if SafetyAlert interface or Vehicle/Teleoperation control loops introduce delays. Mitigation: Define latency budgets; conduct timing analysis; test failover latency via HIL and field trials.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification defining abstract health indicators (motor_wear_index, battery_soc, thermal_margin, subsystem_status) and their measurement/derivation from Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems.
SafetyAlert interface contract specifying failover command format, idempotency guarantees, timeout behavior, and latency requirements (≤100ms).
Tasking interface contract specifying operational context signals (maintenance scheduling, incident alerts) and clarifying that Safety Services does not enforce human actions.
Deterministic failover state machines for each high-consequence failure mode, with formal verification or model-checking results.
FMEA/FTA results identifying high-consequence failure modes and redundancy coverage analysis (target ≥95%).
Predictive maintenance algorithm design and validation results demonstrating accuracy ≥90% on historical Vehicle/Teleoperation health data.
Operational procedures documentation, training program design, and compliance audit framework.

Acceptance criteria for each sub-DP: (1) DP7a: Redundancy coverage ≥95%, failover latency ≤100ms; (2) DP7b: Predictive maintenance accuracy ≥90%, MTBF extension ≥20%; (3) DP7c: Procedure compliance ≥95%, training completion ≥100%, incident response time ≤30 minutes (or customer-defined target).
Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test results validating failover logic with Vehicle and Teleoperation simulators.
Field trial results demonstrating availability ≥0.97 over the agreed evaluation period.
